Name: hardware_optimized_inference
Title: Hardware-Optimized Neural Network Inference for Low-Latency Applications
Short Hypothesis: By leveraging hardware-aware optimization techniques, such as kernel fusion, memory bandwidth optimization, and tensor core occupancy, we can achieve significant reductions in latency and improvements in throughput for neural network inference on commodity GPUs.
Related Work: Recent works, such as AMP-ViT and SNIP, have focused on optimizing vision transformers and large language models for efficient inference. However, these works have primarily concentrated on software-level optimizations, such as adaptive mixed-precision quantization and fine-grained precision selection. In contrast, our proposal focuses on hardware-aware optimization techniques that can be applied to a wide range of neural network architectures.
Abstract: This proposal presents a hardware-optimized approach to neural network inference for low-latency applications. By applying kernel fusion, memory bandwidth optimization, and tensor core occupancy techniques, we aim to reduce latency and improve throughput for neural network inference on commodity GPUs. Our approach is evaluated using a range of benchmarks, including vision transformers and large language models, and is compared to state-of-the-art software-level optimization techniques.
Experiments: Evaluate the effectiveness of kernel fusion in reducing latency and improving throughput for neural network inference.
Investigate the impact of memory bandwidth optimization on neural network inference performance.
Develop and evaluate a tensor core occupancy optimization technique for neural network inference.
Compare the performance of our hardware-optimized approach to state-of-the-art software-level optimization techniques using a range of benchmarks.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The complexity of modern neural network architectures may limit the effectiveness of our hardware-aware optimization techniques.
The need for specialized hardware expertise may pose a barrier to adoption and implementation.
The potential for over-optimization, leading to reduced accuracy or increased energy consumption, must be carefully monitored and mitigated.
